segments.s465-g89: This is a temporal subordinate clause: چو + past verb (بشنید) = 'when he heard'. از ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'from them' that modifies بشنید. فرمان گزید is the main clause joined to that temporal phrase: گزید is past 3rd person singular 'he chose/issued', with فرمان as its object; the verb-final order and the contraction of temporal marker (چو) are typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s465-g90: چنان 'so/such' introduces a result clause; کز is the archaic subordinating 'که' meaning 'that' (not a locative 'from which'). دل شهریاران is a genitive phrase 'the hearts of the rulers/nobles' (poetic word order, ezafe implied), and سزید is a past, intransitive/impersonal form meaning 'was fitting/was due' or 'pleased' — i.e., 'it suited/pleased the hearts'.
segments.s465-g57: The repeated نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...', listing absent items. Word order is simply a noun phrase list: کشور (realm) then لشکر (army/host), then تخت و کلاه (literally 'throne and hat' — here poetic for 'throne and crown'). The conjunction و links تخت and کلاه as a pair.
segments.s465-g58: Continuation of the negative list with نه introducing another series: شاهی 'kingship' (abstract), فرزند 'child/offspring', then گنج 'treasure' and سپاه 'army/host'. The two و's coordinate the nouns; the whole phrase is still under the scope of the initial negation.
segments.s465-g59: ار is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an emphatic/particle (poetic/archaic) that softens or stresses the condition. بخشایش 'forgiveness/mercy' is a noun governed by the following noun کردگار 'the Maker/Creator' (i.e. 'forgiveness of the Creator'). Together they form the conditional subject: 'if the Creator's forgiveness ...'.
segments.s465-g60: نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form 'is not / there is not' following the conditional; the clause then states the consequence: تبه شد 'became ruinous' (تبه as 'ruin/mischief' + شد 'became'). به ما روزگار literally 'to us (our) fate/times' = 'our days/fortune'. The whole clause reads 'if there is no forgiveness from God, our days will become ruined.'
segments.s465-g61: کسی here functions as an indefinite/collective introducer ('those, some') followed by the relative کز (که از) 'who/that from/among'. گرانمایگان is a plural noun 'the illustrious / noble ones', and زیستند is the past plural 'they lived'. Grammatically this yields 'those who lived among the illustrious' or 'some of the illustrious who lived'.
segments.s465-g62: به پیش = 'in the presence of / before', شهنشاه 'the emperor/sovereign' is the indirect object/location, and بگریستند is the past plural 'they wept' (b- prefix + past stem). Together: 'they wept before the king.' Note the verb agrees in number with گرانمایگان.
segments.s465-g63: به آواز literally 'with a voice' = 'aloud/in a loud voice'. گفتند is the past plural 'they said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', here addressing شهریار 'king' — 'O king'. The phrase introduces direct speech.
segments.s465-g64: همه 'all/everyone' + خسته‌ایم is the first-person-plural present 'we are tired' (adjective خسته + personal ending -ایم). از indicates cause 'from/because of', بد روزگار 'bad times/fate' = 'hard times'. The clause is a speaker-inclusive complaint: 'we are all tired of bad times.'
segments.s465-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; the clause has subject سکندر followed by چو + prepositional phrase از کارش and the predicate آگاه شد. کارش is کار + enclitic pronoun ش 'his', so از کارش = 'of his affair(s)'. آگاه شد is past tense 'became aware / learned' (شد as full past verb 'became'). The order is Subject + temporal conjunction + prepositional object + predicate, a normal narrative sequence.
segments.s465-g2: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of what was learned. دارا here functions as the proper name Dāra; به تخت = 'onto the throne' (به as directional 'to/onto'). افسر ماه is a poetic compound: افسر 'helmet/crown' qualified by ماه 'moon' → 'moon‑like diadem/crown'. شد is the past verb 'became / was placed'.
segments.s465-g3: سپه is the direct topic/subject 'the army/host'. برگرفت is a past transitive verb 'took up / raised' (here 'mustered/assembled'). از عراق gives the origin 'from Iraq'. The clause ends with a second verb براند (past 'drove') coordinated by و, so the two verbs share the same subject (he assembled and drove/led the host onward).
segments.s465-g4: به رومی is directional/target 'toward the Romans'. همی + verb (بخواند) is a poetic imperfective/past‑habitual marker: همی indicates repeated or continuous action in narrative and combines with the -د ending. نام یزدان بخواند literally 'he called the name of God' — here 'he invoked the name of God' (بخواند used as 'to call upon/summon').
segments.s465-g5: سپه را uses را as a topical/definite marker ('as for the host'). میان و کرانه 'middle and edge' list two parts/limits; نبود is the past negative copula 'was not / there was not'. Together the clause states that the host had neither middle nor margin — a poetic way to say it lacked order or bounds.
segments.s465-g6: همان 'that very' or 'the same'; بخت here means 'fortune/destiny' (not merely 'luck'); دارا again denotes Dāra; جوانه 'bud' is used metaphorically 'no budding/incipient stage'. نبود is past negative 'was not'. The clause says Dāra's destined success had not even begun to sprout.
segments.s465-g7: پذیره شدن is a deverbal noun phrase 'the reception / to be received' (شدن nominalizes/marks the event), and را marks it as the object/topic. بیاراست is the past transitive verb 'adorned/arranged / made ready' (here 'prepared the reception'). شاه is the subject placed after the verb, a common poetic/narrative word order in which verb precedes subject for emphasis.
segments.s465-g8: بیاورد is past 'he brought'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', so ز اصطخر = 'from Istakhr' (اصطخر is a place-name here). چندان modifies سپاه and means 'so many / such a great number'; سپاه 'army/host' is the object. The clause states that he brought a very large army from Istakhr.
segments.s465-g25: خروشی بلند آمد از بارگاه is a simple nominal phrase + verb: خروشی (a roar/uproar) is the noun, بلند is a post‑nominal adjective meaning 'loud', آمد is the past tense verb 'came/ arose', and از بارگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court/palace' giving the source. Word order (Noun + Adj + Verb + PrepP) is typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s465-g26: که‌ای مهتران نماینده راه is a vocative/exclamatory call. که‌ای (here) functions like an exclamatory 'O', مهتران is the addressed plural 'elders/chiefs', and نماینده راه literally '(be) the ones who show the way' — a call to lead. There is no overt copula; the phrase is an address (imperative sense) to the chiefs to guide the way.
segments.s465-g27: هرانکس که زنهار خواهد همی is a conditional/subordinate clause: هرانکس 'whoever', که introduces the clause, زنهار is a noun meaning 'warning' or 'safety' (an archaic protective noun), خواهد is the classical present 'wants/will', and همی is the classical progressive/emphatic particle attached to the verb. Together: 'whoever wishes protection...'.
segments.s465-g28: ز کرده به یزدان پناهد همی is the main admonition: ز (poetic 'from/against'), کرده here = 'deed' or 'what has been done' (a past participle used as noun), به یزدان 'to/with God', and پناهد همی is an archaic verbal form meaning 'let him seek refuge / he takes refuge' (پناه‑ + subjunctive/optative ending). So the clause tells the hearer to seek God's protection from their deeds.
segments.s465-g29: همه یکسره در پناه منید is a present declaration addressed to the audience: همه 'all', یکسره 'entirely/wholly', در پناه 'in the protection/shelter', and منید is an older/pronominal/verbal cluster where من functions possessively 'my' and the ending ید marks the present/you‑are/are (classical morphology). The whole means 'you are all completely under my protection' (an authoritative present statement).
segments.s465-g30: بدانید اگر نیک‌خواه منید begins with the imperative آرام/command بدانید 'know (this)', اگر 'if / provided that', followed by نیک‌خواه 'well‑wisher' and again منید in the classical sense 'you are (my)' — so the sense is 'Know this, if you are my well‑wishers.' The clause uses an imperative verb to call for attention before the conditional.
segments.s465-g31: همه خستگان را ببخشیم چیز is a clause with object marking then verb: همه خستگان را 'all the weary' (را marks the definite object), ببخشیم is first‑person plural hortative/optative or future‑oriented 'we will forgive / let us forgive', and چیز here is a short particle/connector meaning something like 'for that matter' or 'this matter' (literally 'thing'). The clause announces clemency toward the exhausted.
segments.s465-g32: همان خون دشمن نریزیم نیز states the complementary pledge: همان 'that same / exactly that', خون دشمن 'the enemy's blood' (blood of the enemy), نریزیم is the negative first‑person plural form (let us not / we will not shed — classical subjunctive/optative/negative), and نیز 'also/likewise' ties it to the previous clause. Together: 'and likewise we will not spill the enemy's blood.'
segments.s465-g81: Imperative clause with subject 'تو' (poetic 'thou') understood as the addressee. 'او را' is the definite object marked by the enclitic را. 'به تن' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in (his) body / personally', and 'زیردستی' names the status ('subordination') he should show. 'نمای' is the 2nd-person singular imperative of 'نمودن/نما' meaning 'make appear / show'.
segments.s465-g82: 'یکی' here is an indefinite/quantifying element ('a bit' / 'one (certain) thing'); 'در سخن' is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in speech', 'نیز' = 'also'. 'چربی' literally 'fat' is used metaphorically for smoothing/flattery in speech, and 'فزای' is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning 'cause to increase / add'. The whole segment instructs to add some flattering smoothness to the person's words.
segments.s465-g83: 'ببینیم' is 1st-person plural jussive/subjunctive ('let us see' / 'we will see'). 'فرجام' is the object ('the outcome'). 'تا' here introduces the subordinate clause that follows ('as to / so that / to indicate the manner'), and 'چون' = 'how'. 'بود' is the past tense copula ('was'). The clause reads as an invitation to examine how the outcome was.
segments.s465-g84: 'که' functions as a causal conjunction ('for / because'). 'گردش' is the subject ('the turning / turn (of fate/events)'), 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from / of', 'اندیشه' = 'thought', 'بیرون' = 'outside', and 'بود' is the past copula ('was'). Together: 'for the turning was outside (beyond) thought.'
segments.s465-g85: 'یکی' is an indefinite pronoun here ('someone / a certain person') indicating that a person (not specified) should act. 'نامه بنویس' is an imperative ('write a letter' or more idiomatically 'have a letter written'). 'نزدیک او' specifies the recipient/agent as 'near him' (i.e., to someone close to him or in his vicinity). The overall sense is an instruction to employ someone close to the man to write/deliver a letter.
segments.s465-g86: 'پراندیشه' is an adjectival/participial compound meaning 'full of thought / thoughtful (or careworn)'. Combined with the causative/impositive verb 'کن' (imperative 'make/do'), it forms a causative command: 'make (his) ... thoughtful.' 'جان تاریک او' is the object phrase 'his dark/gloomy soul/heart' with possessive 'او' following the noun. So: 'make his dark soul thoughtful.'
segments.s465-g87: 'هم' = 'also / even', emphasizing the statement. 'این چرخ گردان' is a nominal phrase 'this revolving wheel' (a common poetic image for fate/heaven). The two verbs 'برو بگذرد' form a verbal sequence where the first ('برو' = 'go') adds motion/emphasis and the second ('بگذرد' = 'passes / will pass') is the main predicate; together they mean 'will go by / will pass away' (expressing inevitable change). The verb form is presented as a general/future inevitability rather than a simple present.
segments.s465-g88: 'چنین' = 'thus / so' introduces the comment. 'داند' is the present tense verb 'knows' (used here as a general truth). 'آنکس که دارد خرد' is a relative clause 'the one who has wisdom' (آنکس = 'that person', که = relative 'who', دارد = 'has', خرد = 'wisdom'). Word order is verb before subject for rhetorical emphasis: 'Thus knows he who has wisdom.'
segments.s465-g33: ز + چیز کسان = a prepositional phrase 'from/about the things of people'; دست کوته کردن is an idiom meaning 'to shorten one's hand' = 'to hold back one's hand / refrain from taking'. کن��م (کردن in the 1st pl.) is used here as a hortative/volitive 'let us (do)'. Word order places the prepositional object before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s465-g34: خرد را: خرد (wisdom) marked by the object marker را. سوی روشنی = prepositional phrase 'toward the light/clarity'; ره is the archaic/poetic form of راه 'way'. کن��م again is the 1st plural volitive 'let us set/lead (it)'. The clause therefore means 'let us put/lead wisdom onto the way of light.'
segments.s465-g35: که is the relative/complementizer 'that / who' introducing the clause. پیروزگر is the agent 'the victor / triumphant one'. دادمان is a past/perfective verb 'gave / bestowed (to us)' (داد + مان = 'gave us'). فرهی is the direct object/adjective meaning 'noble / cultured' describing what was given.
segments.s465-g36: Two nouns joined by و: بزرگی و دیهیم form a coordinated phrase describing qualities given, followed by شاهنشهی which specifies these qualities as belonging to kingship (i.e. 'the greatness and ... of the imperial throne'). دیهیم is an archaic/poetic epithet here; it modifies بزرگی and together they are in apposition to شاهنشهی.
segments.s465-g37: کسی 'someone' + کو: here a classical/poetic relative 'who/that' (not the modern interrogative 'where'); ز فرمان ما 'from our command' (ز = from), بگذرد is used in the sense 'to go beyond / transgress' (present/subjunctive form used in a conditional sense: 'if anyone should transgress our command').
segments.s465-g38: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'indeed / surely'. گردن اژدها is the direct object 'the dragon's neck' (possessive/genitive relationship). بشکرد is an archaic/perfective verb form 'to break/shatter' placed at the end (verb-final order is common in poetry); together the clause threatens physical punishment for disobedience.
segments.s465-g39: ز چیزی = 'concerning / because of something'; که introduces a relative clause modifying چیزی. دید اندران رزمگاه is a compact relative 'he saw among them in the battlefield' (دید = 'saw' in past tense; اندران = اندر + ان = 'among them / therein'; رزمگاه = 'battlefield'). The whole phrase = 'because of something that he saw there on the battlefield.'
segments.s465-g40: ببخشید is a past tense verb 'he pardoned / forgave' (ب- prefix with بخشید). یکسر is an adverb 'at once / entirely'; همه 'all/everyone'; بر سپاه 'upon the army' (بر = on/over/against used here to mark the target 'the army'). Word order places the verb before its adverbial and object for emphasis: 'he pardoned, all at once, upon the army.'
segments.s465-g9: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). گفتی here is the 2nd-person past ('you said') introducing reported speech. ستاره is the subject 'star', and نتابد is the 3rd-person present (classical) negative form 'does not shine' (ن- prefix + تابد); همی is the old/dialectal durative/emphatic particle attached after the verb to give a continuous or emphatic sense ('ever/at all').
segments.s465-g10: This clause names the subject (فلک, 'the heavens/sky') and describes inability: راه رفتن is a noun phrase 'a way/road of going' or 'a way to move' (راه + the verbal noun/infinitive رفتن). نیابد is the 3rd-person present negative of یافتن ('does not find / cannot find'). همی again gives a durative/emphatic flavour, so the whole means the heavens cannot find a way to move.
segments.s465-g11: سپاه 'army/host' is the noun; دو کشور is a genitival phrase 'of the two countries' modifying سپاه. کشیدند is past plural 'they drew' (here 'drew up' a line/formed a rank) — verb follows the subject in this classical poetic order, and صف is the object 'row/formation' (the battle line they drew).
segments.s465-g12: همه 'all' quantifies the fighters; the list نیزه و گرز و خنجر enumerates weapons (spear, mace, dagger). به + کف forms a prepositional phrase meaning 'in (their) hand' or 'at hand' (به here marks relation/towards and کف 'palm/hand' names the location).
segments.s465-g13: برآمد is the past singular 'arose/issued forth' (verb first, as typical in poetry). چنان 'such/so' intensifies the following noun. از دو لشکر marks source 'from the two armies', and خروش is the noun 'a cry/roar' which is what rose.
segments.s465-g14: که introduces a result clause ('so that'). چرخ فلک is a compound meaning 'the vault of heaven / the celestial sphere' (both words point to sky/heaven). را is the definite object marker making گوش the definite object; بدرید is the past plural (or past) of دریدن 'to tear' ('they/it tore'), and گوش is 'ear' (here used figuratively of the heavens' ear).
segments.s465-g15: چو is the subordinating particle 'when'. دریا 'sea' is the predicate noun and شد is the past 'became' (چو ... شد = 'when ... became'). از خون 'from/of blood' gives the material causing the sea metaphor, and گردان زمین 'the troop/host of the earth' is the subject phrase (literal 'the earth's troop/host').
segments.s465-g16: تن 'body' with بی‌سران 'of the headless' forms 'the bodies of the headless'. بد here describes those bodies as wretched/dreadful; همه 'all' quantifies, and دشت کین is a genitive phrase 'the plain/field of vengeance' — together the clause says the plain was full of the headless bodies, the whole field being one of vengeance.
segments.s465-g65: 'سپه را' places 'سپه' (the army) in focus with the enclitic 'را' (here functioning as a topical/emphatic marker rather than a modern direct‑object only marker). 'ز کوشش' = 'because of toil/effort' (ز used in the causal sense). 'سخن درگذشت' is a poetic formula: 'sokhan' (speech/words) + past 'درگذشت' = 'words gave out / speech ceased' (i.e., there was nothing more to say).
segments.s465-g66: This clause is ordered with the prepositional source 'ز ...' before the verb at the end. 'ز' here marks source/location ('from/of'); 'تارک' is an adjective/participial sense ('deserted'/'departed') modifying the following noun; 'دم آب' = 'the edge/tip of the water' (دم here = edge/tip); 'برتر گذشت' places the adjective 'برتر' ('higher/surpassing') immediately before the verb 'گذشت' (past 'passed'), yielding 'passed beyond / surpassed'. The verb comes sentence‑final as in classical Persian.
segments.s465-g67: 'پدر بی‌پسر شد' is a simple past clause: 'the father became sonless' (شد = became). The following nominal phrase 'پسر بی‌پدر' omits the verb by ellipsis and repeats the parallel idea ('the son (became) fatherless'); this kind of terse parallelism is common in epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s465-g68: 'چنین آمد' = 'thus befell / thus it happened' (آمد here with the sense 'happen'). The prepositional phrase 'از چرخ گردان' ('from the revolving wheel') names fate/the wheel of fortune (چرخ = fate/cycle, گردان = revolving). 'به سر' ('upon the head') uses به + noun to indicate what is afflicted/affected — i.e. 'it came upon his head.'
segments.s465-g69: 'کرا' is an archaic relative/indefinite pronoun meaning 'whoever' or 'who'; it introduces the list 'مادر و خواهر و دختر است' = 'has a mother and sister and daughter' with the copula 'است' at the end. Word order here (pronoun + list + است) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s465-g70: 'همه پاک' is an emphatic/adverbial fronting meaning 'all entirely' (پاک used adverbially). 'بر دست اسکندر است' is an idiomatic stative: بر + 'دست' = 'in the hand/power of'; with 'اسکندر' as possessor and 'است' as copula, the phrase means 'are wholly in Alexander's power.'
segments.s465-g71: 'همان' = 'those very / the same'; 'پاک' is adjectival 'pure' modifying the following plural noun. 'پوشیده‌رویان' is a plural agentive/adjective meaning 'veiled‑faced' or 'modest ones' (the -ان plural); 'تو' placed after the noun marks possession in classical word order ('of yours').
segments.s465-g72: This is a relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that'). 'بودند' is the past plural 'they were.' 'لرزنده' is a present participle/adjectival participle 'trembling'; 'بر جان تو' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ('for/on your life' = 'anxious/fearful for you'). Together: 'who were trembling for your life.'
segments.s465-g73: 'چو' here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. 'گنج نیاکان' is a genitive phrase 'treasure of the ancestors' (noun noun order common in older/poetic Persian), and 'برترمنش' is an adjective/postpositive modifier ('proud'/'high-minded') qualifying 'نیاکان'. Word order is essentially 'when [treasure of the proud ancestors] ...'.
segments.s465-g74: 'که' introduces the relative/finite clause attached to the temporal 'چو' ('which/that / when'). 'آمد' is a simple past/present-stative verb 'came'. 'به دست تو' is a prepositional / locative phrase 'into/into the hand of you' (note the possessive is expressed as 'دست تو' rather than the ezāfe form, a poetic ordering). 'بی‌سرزنش' is a compound adverbial/nominal phrase 'without reproach' (بی- = without + سرزنش) describing the manner.
segments.s465-g75: 'کنون' is the temporal adverb 'now' placed at the clause front. 'مانده' is the participial/predicative form 'remains/has remained' serving as the main predicate. 'اندر' is an archaic preposition 'in/within', and 'کف رومیان' ('the palm/hand of the Romans') is the locative phrase marking where those things remain; note the genitive follows the noun as is typical in classical verse.
segments.s465-g76: 'نژاد بزرگان' is a noun + modifier construction meaning 'the lineage (or race) of the great ones' (بزرگان = 'the great/elders'). The conjunction 'و' links this to the parallel noun phrase 'گنج کیان' ('the treasure of the Kians' — Kian = royal house/kings). These two coordinated noun phrases are the syntactic subjects/objects of 'مانده' in the previous segment.
segments.s465-g77: 'ترا' (here split orthographically into 'ت' + 'را') functions as the 2nd-person object/possessive marker 'your'/'to you' and precedes the noun in older/poetic order, so 'ترا چاره' = 'your remedy' or 'the remedy for you'. 'با او' means 'with him'. 'مدارا' (appearing as 'مداراست' with the copula attached) is a nominal predicate 'forbearance/tolerance is'. The final 'بس' is an interjection/particle 'enough' (i.e. 'that is enough'/'stop there').
segments.s465-g78: 'که' here introduces the explanatory/casual clause 'for/because'. 'تاج بزرگی' is 'a crown of greatness' or 'the great crown' (تاج modified by بزرگی). 'نماند' is the negative stative verb 'does not remain / is not left' (used as a general statement). 'به کس' is the dative/prepositional phrase 'for anyone' (literally 'to/for any person'). Together this clause gives the reason for 'بس'.
segments.s465-g79: 'کسی گوید' is a conventional introductory formula 'someone says' or 'one might say' (گوید is the present/habitual-reporting verb used to introduce reported speech). 'آتش زبانش' is a genitive expression 'the fire of his tongue' — a figurative phrase about his speech — where 'زبان' functions as 'speech/utterance' here. 'نسوخت' is the negative past/predicative 'did not burn' (setting up a reported objection or claim).
segments.s465-g80: 'به چاره بد' literally 'by/with a bad remedy' ('به' marking the instrument/means). 'از تن' is the source phrase 'from the body'. The verbal phrase 'بباید سپوخت' is impersonal/obligative: 'one must burn' or 'it is necessary to burn' — بایَد (بباید) + subjunctive/inflected form سپوخت expresses obligation or necessity. Together: 'by a bad remedy, one must burn from the body' (i.e. suffer bodily harm because of a bad cure).
segments.s465-g17: پدر را (pader râ) uses را as the topical/definite marker on پدر ("the father"). نبد is an archaic past negative/existential (= نبَود), meaning "was not" or "did not exist." بر پسر جای مهر is a prepositional phrase where بر + noun expresses direction/toward ("toward the son") and جای مهر = "a place/position of affection" (جای = "place"; مهر = "affection/love"). Together: 'There was no place of fatherly affection toward the son.'
segments.s465-g18: بریشان is the fused form of بر + ایشان, i.e. "to/for them" (بر meaning "toward/to"). نبخشید is past tense of بخشیدن used here in the sense "did not give / did not bestow." گردان is adjectival/epithet meaning "revolving" modifying سپهر (the heavens), so گردان سپهر = "the revolving heavens." The clause says the heavens did not grant that fatherly affection.
segments.s465-g19: سیم ره literally "silver way" (سیم + ره) is a compound describing the path. به دارا indicates movement toward/into دارا (here probably the name Dara or a wealthy place). درآمد is past tense "entered/arrived" (came in). شکست is here used as a verbal/result meaning "broke/was broken" or "was broken apart." The clause: the silver way reached Dara and broke.
segments.s465-g20: میان is an adverb/adverbial noun here meaning "in the middle (of)"; تاختن is the masdar/infinitive "to charge/gallop" used as the object (action) and is marked by را as definite object. ببست is the past tense "he closed/blocked." Grammatically: 'Alexander closed/blocked the charge in mid-course.'
segments.s465-g21: جهاندار is a title formed from جهان+دار, meaning "sovereign/World-holder." لشکر is the object "army/host." به + place (کرمان) indicates direction "to Kerman." کشید is the past tense of کشیدن; here it has the sense 'drew/led/moved (an army)' — a transitive action taking لشکر as its object and به کرمان as its destination.
segments.s465-g22: همی is an older adverb meaning "continually/still" introducing the ongoing action. از بد دشمنان is a prepositional phrase "from the wicked/bad enemies." جان here means "life." کشید repeats کشیدن in past tense; in this context it conveys "he exacted/took (life)" from the enemies (i.e. he slew them or inflicted mortal harm).
segments.s465-g23: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). بیامد is simple past "came." زی is an archaic preposition meaning "from/of," so زی اصطخر پارس = "from Istakhr of Pars" (اصطخر is the place name Istakhr, پارس = Pars). Word order places the origin after the verb, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s465-g24: که introduces a descriptive/relative clause about the preceding subject. دیهیم is an older adjective/noun meaning "rural/peasant-like" or "country-dweller," used here as an epithet before شاهان (kings) — the phrase qualifies the person as a kind of rustic ruler. بد is the adjective "bad/harsh," و is the conjunction "and," and فخر پارس is the compound "the pride/glory of Pars." The clause as a whole gives contrasting epithets: rustic and harsh, yet the pride of Pars.
segments.s465-g49: چنین (an adverb) is fronted for emphasis: 'thus/so'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (verb + subject inversion is common in poetic Persian: 'چنین گفت دارا' = 'thus said Dara'). که introduces the reported clause that follows, and هم بی‌گمان is an adverbial intensifier ('indeed / even undoubtedly') modifying the subordinate clause.
segments.s465-g50: This is a compact predicative clause with an inverted word order typical of classical/poetic Persian: 'ز ما' (here causal: 'because of / on our account'), followed by the copula بود ('was' — past), then the predicate phrase 'بد آسمان' ('bad/hostile heaven'), and the locative/target phrase 'بر ما' ('upon/against us'). Together: 'because of us the hostile heaven was upon us.'
segments.s465-g51: شکن is a noun 'breaker/tormentor' placed before a genitive phrase 'زین نشان' ('of this sign/token'); the prepositional phrase 'در جهان' ('in the world') sets the domain, and the verb ندید ('did not see') comes at the end — a standard SOV order in poetry. The overall meaning: 'No one in the world saw such a breaker of this sign.'
segments.s465-g52: نه ... شنید is a negative continuation: 'nor did (they) hear'. از کاردانان پیشین is an ablative/source phrase 'from the earlier experts' (کاردانان = skilled/learned people, پیشین = former/ancient). Together: 'nor did any of the former experts hear of it.'
segments.s465-g53: زن و کودک is a coordinated noun phrase 'women/wives and children' modified by the genitive شهریاران ('of the lords/kings'). اسیر is a nominal predicate 'captive' (the copula is omitted, as is common), so: 'the lords' wives and children (were) captives.'
segments.s465-g54: وگر introduces an alternative/conditional ('and/or if / but if'). کشته is a past participle/adjective 'killed/slain', followed by خسته 'worn out/exhausted' as a descriptive phrase. به + nouns (ژوپین و تیر) gives instruments/circumstances ('by/with gambesons and arrows' — i.e. their jackets were pierced and arrows were involved). The line lists fates: captive, or slain and spent, pierced through their coats by arrows.
segments.s465-g55: This is an interrogative: چه بینید = 'what do you see' (چه = 'what', بینید = 2nd-person-plural present/subjunctive form used here interrogatively). و این را چه درمان کنید = 'and what remedy will you do for/with this' — این را is the definite object 'this', درمان is 'remedy', and کنید (2pl) asks what action you will take. The structure asks both for observation and for a practical solution.
segments.s465-g56: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'): 'that you make the ill-wisher regret it.' بدخواه را is the marked object ('the ill-wisher' + rā), زین is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'از این' ('for/from this' = 'about this') functioning as the thing causing regret, پشیمان is the adjective 'regretful', and کنید is the 2nd-person plural causative/action verb 'you make/do'.
segments.s465-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the time clause. دارا is the proper name (here presented as the lexical form 'دارا'). ز ... به ... is the poetic order for 'from X to Y' (ز ایران به کرمان). رسید is simple past, 3rd-person singular 'arrived/reached', finishing the temporal clause.
segments.s465-g42: This clause contains the negated perception: ندید is past 'did not see' (here with third-person plural implied from context or narrative subject). دو بهر is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'either of the two' / 'both of the two' in classical usage; از بزرگان لشکر names the group 'of the great ones of the army.' The word order puts the quantified phrase before the source (از ...).
segments.s465-g43: This is a nominal phrase describing scene: خروشی 'a roar/clamor' qualified by بد 'ill/ominous' and located by the archaic preposition اندر 'in/within' plus میان 'the middle of'. This is an in situ descriptive clause rather than a full finite clause (typical of epic style).
segments.s465-g44: This clause has ندیدند 'they did not see' (past, 3rd-person plural). یکی را is the indefinite object 'anyone' marked with the definite/object marker را, and بر سر کلاه is a prepositional phrase 'on the head [a] hat' (i.e. 'wearing a hat on the head'). Word order places the object before the verb, and the locative phrase after it.
segments.s465-g45: This is a transitive past clause: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' is the subject, فرزانه را 'the wise (men)' is the direct object (را marks the object), and گرد کرد literally 'made (them) about/around' — a common classical compound meaning 'assembled' or 'gathered about.' کرد is past 'did/made'.
segments.s465-g46: کسی را is an object phrase 'someone' (را marks it). The relative clause introduced by که ('who/that') modifies کسی: با او 'with him' and بد اندر نبرد is an idiomatic classical sequence meaning 'in ill/bitter battle' or simply 'in battle against him.' There is no new finite verb here because this relative clause qualifies the noun 'کسی.'
segments.s465-g47: همه 'all' is a universal quantifier; مهتران (here 'chiefs' or 'elders') is the plural subject. زار و گریان شدند is a compound predicate: زار 'wretched/miserable' and گریان 'weeping' are predicative adjectives/nouns linked by و, followed by شدند (past plural of شدن) 'became' — a standard Persian copular/auxiliary construction.
segments.s465-g48: This clause gives the cause/result: ز بخت بد خویش is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of their own ill fate' (ز = 'from/because of'; خوی + ش is 'their own [disposition/fate]'). بریان شدند is a metaphorical passive/resultative 'were burnt/consumed' (i.e. overwhelmed/consumed by it). شدند is past plural 'became/were.'

sentences.s465-s45: When he heard that from them, he issued such an order that it pleased the hearts of the nobles.
sentences.s465-s29: There is neither realm nor host, neither throne nor crown; no kingship, no offspring, nor treasure or army.
sentences.s465-s30: If there is no forgiveness from the Creator, our days will be ruined.
sentences.s465-s31: Those of the illustrious who had lived (or lived) before the king wept.
sentences.s465-s32: They said aloud, 'O king, we are all weary of hard times.'
sentences.s465-s1: When Alexander learned of the matter — that Dāra had been seated on the throne with the moon‑like diadem.
sentences.s465-s2: He mustered an army from Iraq and drove it forth; toward the Romans he continually called on the name of God.
sentences.s465-s3: The host had neither center nor edge; that very fortune of Dāra had not yet budded.
sentences.s465-s4: The king put the reception in order, and from Istakhr he brought so many troops.
sentences.s465-s13: A loud cry came from the palace: "O chiefs, show (us) the way!"
sentences.s465-s14: Whoever desires a warning (or safety), let him take refuge with God from (his) deeds.
sentences.s465-s15: All of you are wholly under my protection — know this, if you are my well‑wishers.
sentences.s465-s16: We will pardon all the weary; likewise, we will not shed the enemy's blood.
sentences.s465-s41: Make him appear as a subordinate in person; and in speech also increase some smoothness (flattery).
sentences.s465-s42: Let us see the outcome — how it was, for the turning (of events/fate) lay beyond thought.
sentences.s465-s43: Have someone write a letter to a person near him; make his dark soul full of thought.
sentences.s465-s44: This revolving wheel too will pass away; so knows the one who possesses wisdom.
sentences.s465-s17: Let us refrain from taking people's goods, and set wisdom on the path toward the light.
sentences.s465-s18: that the victorious one bestowed upon us noble greatness and the majesty of kingship.
sentences.s465-s19: If anyone departs from our command, we will break the dragon's neck.
sentences.s465-s20: Because of something he saw in that battlefield, he pardoned the whole army at once.
sentences.s465-s5: You said the star will not shine, and the heavens will not find a way to move.
sentences.s465-s6: The armies of the two countries drew up in ranks; all had spears, maces, and daggers in hand.
sentences.s465-s7: Such a cry rose from the two hosts that it tore the heavens' ear.
sentences.s465-s8: When the earth's host became a sea of blood, the plain was strewn with the bodies of the headless — the whole field a place of vengeance.
sentences.s465-s33: Words failed the army from its toil; he passed beyond the water's brink in a breath.
sentences.s465-s34: The father became without a son, the son without a father — thus it came from the revolving wheel upon the head.
sentences.s465-s35: Whoever has a mother and a sister and a daughter — all are entirely in Alexander's hand.
sentences.s465-s36: Those same pure, veiled‑faced ones of yours, who used to tremble for your life.
sentences.s465-s37: When the proud ancestral treasure came into your hand without reproach.
sentences.s465-s38: Now the lineage of the great and the Kian treasure remain in the palm of the Romans.
sentences.s465-s39: Make your remedy with him forbearance — enough; for no crown of greatness remains to anyone.
sentences.s465-s40: Some say, 'The fire of his tongue did not burn; because of a bad remedy, one must burn from the body.'
sentences.s465-s9: There was no fatherly place of affection for the son; the revolving heavens did not bestow it upon them.
sentences.s465-s10: The silver way reached Dara and was broken; Alexander cut off (blocked) the charge in mid-course.
sentences.s465-s11: The sovereign drew his host to Kerman; continually he exacted life from the wicked enemies.
sentences.s465-s12: Alexander came from Istakhr in Pars; he was a rustic (country) ruler, harsh and the pride of Pars.
sentences.s465-s25: Dara said thus: indeed, without doubt the hostile heaven was against us.
sentences.s465-s26: No one in the world had seen such a breaker of this token, nor had any of the former experts heard of it.
sentences.s465-s27: The lords' wives and children were taken captive; and where slain they lay spent, their gambesons pierced by arrows.
sentences.s465-s28: What do you see, and what remedy will you apply for this, so that you make the ill-wisher regret it?
sentences.s465-s21: When Dārā arrived in Kerman from Iran, he did not see either of the two leading commanders of the army.
sentences.s465-s22: There was an ominous clamor in the midst of the army; they saw no one with a hat on his head.
sentences.s465-s23: The elders gathered the sages around them and (looked for) someone who had fought with him in battle.
sentences.s465-s24: All the chiefs became miserable and wept; they were consumed by their own ill fortune.
